title: Chicago to Santa Monica (Route 66 Centennial)
subtitle: 2,448 miles by road
script: Did you know for about 315 dollars in gas you could drive 2,448 miles from Chicago all the way to Santa Monica? That's the entire length of Route 66, which turns 100 years old in 2026. You start under the sign on Adams Street in Chicago, chase the sun through St. Louis and its Gateway Arch, then hit Tulsa's neon and the graffiti of Cactus Ranch in Amarillo. By Flagstaff you're a mile high in pine country, and then you drop down into California until the road literally ends at the Santa Monica Pier. Eight states, one century of American asphalt, and every diner and motel is throwing a centennial party this year. Would you drive the whole Mother Road, or just the best 200 miles of it?
whyTrending: U.S. Route 66 celebrates its 100th anniversary in 2026, with all eight states hosting centennial festivals, car shows, and monument unveilings from spring through fall. Flagstaff's June 6, 2026 celebration and the Historic Route 66 Fun Run are drawing global visitors. It is the single most searched American road trip of the year.

title: Las Vegas to Zion (Hidden Side of the West)
subtitle: 165 miles by road
waypoints: Valley of Fire State Park, NV
script: 165 miles from Las Vegas to Zion, and it costs you just 20 bucks of gas. Rate this route out of 10 before you even leave the Strip. In under three hours you go from Las Vegas casinos to the red sandstone of Valley of Fire, where the rock looks like it's actually on fire at sunset. You roll into Springdale for tacos, then the canyon walls close in and you're standing under Zion's 2,000-foot cliffs. This is the exact stretch RVshare named the number one trending road trip of 2026, the so-called Hidden Side of the West. Fall is prime season because the summer crowds thin out after Labor Day. So what's your score, and are you actually booking it?
whyTrending: RVshare named the Hidden Side of the West the number one trending road trip for 2026, threading Nevada into Utah through underrated parks. Zion sees its crowds drop sharply after Labor Day, making fall the insider season. The 2026 travel surge tied to America's 250th birthday is pushing Southwest park visits higher.

title: San Francisco to Los Angeles (Pacific Coast Highway)
subtitle: 450 miles by road
script: For about 60 dollars of gas you can cross 450 miles from San Francisco to Los Angeles on the most photographed coastline in America. Big Sur reopened to through-traffic in January 2026, which means you can finally drive the full Pacific Coast Highway again for the first time in years. From San Francisco you hug the cliffs south through Monterey's aquarium and 17-Mile Drive. Big Sur is the part people cry over, ocean on your right and the Santa Lucia mountains on your left. You stop at San Simeon for the elephant seals and Hearst Castle, cruise Santa Barbara's palm-lined waterfront, and finish in LA. Is this the best drive in the country, yes or no?
whyTrending: The Big Sur stretch of Highway 1 reopened to through-traffic in January 2026 after landslide closures, restoring the full Pacific Coast Highway for the first time in years. Pent-up demand and reopening coverage have made it one of the most searched 2026 drives. Spring and early summer bring clear coastal skies before fog season.

title: Boston to Bar Harbor (New England Fall Foliage)
subtitle: 280 miles by road
script: You could drive from Boston all the way to Bar Harbor, 280 miles, on about 35 dollars of gas. New England peaks for fall color in the first week of October, and this run catches all of it. You leave Boston, cross into New Hampshire for lobster rolls on the Portsmouth waterfront, then follow the Maine coast north. Portland gives you working lighthouses and a food scene that punches way above its size, Camden hands you a harbor framed in red maples, and you end at Bar Harbor where Acadia National Park meets the Atlantic. The 2026 forecast points to a warm early autumn, so peak leaves may hit right on schedule. Where are you starting your leaf-peeping this fall?
whyTrending: The 2026 New England fall foliage forecast points to peak color breaking out in early October, with a warm early autumn keeping the season roughly on schedule. Acadia National Park and the mid-coast Maine harbors are among the most photographed leaf-peeping destinations. October coastal Maine lodging books out months ahead.

title: Asheville to Cherokee (Blue Ridge Parkway)
subtitle: 165 miles by road
script: 165 miles from Asheville to Cherokee, and it runs you just 20 dollars in gas. Rate America's favorite drive out of 10. The Blue Ridge Parkway holds a 45 mile-per-hour speed limit on purpose, because you're supposed to slow down and stare. From Asheville you climb to Mount Mitchell, the highest peak east of the Mississippi at 6,684 feet, roll through the artsy town of Blowing Rock, and stop at Waterrock Knob for a valley view that goes 50 miles deep. Peak leaf color hits these elevations mid-October, but book early because western North Carolina hotels sell out by August. Some sections are still recovering from Hurricane Helene, so check openings. What's your score for the Parkway?
whyTrending: The Blue Ridge Parkway hits peak fall color at higher elevations in mid-October, and western North Carolina lodging routinely sells out by August. Recovery from 2024's Hurricane Helene has kept the route in the news, with travelers checking section reopenings for 2026. It remains one of the most searched American scenic drives every autumn.

title: Miami to Key West (Overseas Highway)
subtitle: 160 miles by road
script: For about 20 dollars in gas you can cross 160 miles from Miami to Key West, and one stretch is a bridge 7 miles long with turquoise water on both sides and nothing else. That's the Overseas Highway, and it turns 42 bridges into one road hopping island to island. You dive the coral reefs at Key Largo, the only living barrier reef in the continental US, eat fresh mahi in Islamorada, cross the Seven Mile Bridge at Marathon, and end at the southernmost point in the continental US, just 90 miles from Cuba. April is the sweet spot, warm water and way fewer crowds than the packed winter high season. The whole thing is just bridges and islands. Would you road-trip across the ocean like this?
whyTrending: The Overseas Highway is at its best in April, when the Keys are warm, less humid, and far less crowded than the winter high season. The Seven Mile Bridge and southernmost-point photo ops are perennial viral content. Spring break shoulder timing makes it a top-searched 2026 escape.

title: Durango to Telluride (San Juan Skyway)
subtitle: 230 miles by road
script: 230 miles from Durango to Telluride, and it costs you just 30 dollars in gas. They call this the Million Dollar Highway, and one stretch has no guardrails with a thousand-foot drop right off the edge. The San Juan Skyway loops through Colorado's southwest corner, and this section from Durango is the wildest. You climb over three separate mountain passes above 10,000 feet, hit the old silver-mining town of Silverton, drop into Ouray, the so-called Switzerland of America with hot springs right in town, and finish in Telluride's dead-end box canyon. Late September lights the aspens up solid gold across these slopes, which is exactly when you want to be here. Could you handle driving a mountain highway with no guardrails?
whyTrending: The San Juan Skyway's Million Dollar Highway explodes with golden aspens in late September, drawing peak leaf-peeping traffic to Ouray, Silverton, and Telluride. Colorado's byway season and Telluride's fall festivals make it a top autumn draw. The guardrail-free cliff sections are viral staples on travel feeds.

title: Moab to Arches and Canyonlands (Utah Mighty 5 start)
subtitle: 95 miles by road
waypoints: Dead Horse Point State Park, UT
script: You could drive this whole 95-mile loop from Moab to Monticello on about 10 dollars of gas. Arches National Park has over 2,000 natural stone arches, and this route hits the two most famous in a single day. Start in Moab, the adventure capital of Utah, drive into Arches for Delicate Arch, the one on the license plate. Then swing over to Dead Horse Point, where the Colorado River makes a sweeping 2,000-foot horseshoe on the canyon floor below you, and finish in Canyonlands, which is basically the Grand Canyon but with almost nobody in it. Spring and fall are the move here because summer hits 105 degrees on the bare slickrock. This is the anchor stop of Utah's famous Mighty 5. How many of the five have you actually done?
whyTrending: Utah's Mighty 5 park loop is a bucket-list 2026 driver, and Arches plus Canyonlands anchor its Moab hub. Spring and fall are ideal as summer slickrock temperatures top 100 degrees. America's 250th-birthday travel year is pushing record national-park visitation across the Southwest.

title: Seattle to Mount Rainier and Olympic
subtitle: 210 miles by road
script: For about 25 dollars in gas you can drive 210 miles from Seattle to Port Angeles and stand in a rainforest and a glacier zone on the same day. Only Washington lets you do it. From Seattle you drive to Mount Rainier, a 14,411-foot volcano wrapped in wildflower meadows called Paradise. Then you cut west past the state capital in Olympia, into the Hoh area near Lake Quinault where moss hangs off every tree in an actual temperate rainforest, and end at Port Angeles right on the edge of Olympic National Park. Two national parks, one loop. July and August are the only months Rainier's high Paradise roads are fully snow-free, so your timing really matters here. Rainforest or glacier, which one are you hiking first?
whyTrending: The Pacific Northwest national-park circuit peaks from July to August when Mount Rainier's Paradise wildflower meadows bloom and high roads clear of snow. Olympic's Hoh rainforest offers the rare rainforest-to-glacier contrast in one drive. PNW park road trips are a top 2026 summer search.

title: New Orleans to Memphis (Great River Road)
subtitle: 400 miles by road
script: Did you know for about 50 dollars in gas you could drive 400 miles from New Orleans to Memphis? The Great River Road just got voted the best scenic drive in America for 2026, and this is the deep-south stretch of it. You start with beignets in New Orleans, roll up to Baton Rouge past antebellum plantations, hit Natchez where mansions sit right on the bluffs above the Mississippi, then Vicksburg with its Civil War battlefield, and finish on Beale Street in Memphis. That's blues, barbecue, and river history the whole way. Spring means mild weather and everything green before summer humidity slams the delta. The full route runs 10 states, but this is the soul of it. Would you drive the whole Mississippi someday?
whyTrending: The Great River Road was voted Best Scenic Drive in America in the 2026 USA Today 10Best awards, spotlighting its 10-state route along the Mississippi. The southern stretch pairs music history in New Orleans and Memphis with antebellum river towns. Spring offers mild weather before delta humidity peaks.

title: Fredericksburg to Austin (Texas Hill Country Bluebonnets)
subtitle: 90 miles by road
waypoints: Enchanted Rock State Natural Area, TX
script: For about 10 dollars in gas, you can drive the 90 miles from Fredericksburg to Austin, and for six weeks every spring the whole route turns solid blue. 2026's bluebonnet peak lands in late March. Start in Fredericksburg, the German wine town, and climb Enchanted Rock, a giant pink granite dome you can hike in an hour. Roll through Johnson City past LBJ's ranch, hit Dripping Springs, the unofficial wedding capital of Texas with its clear-water swimming holes, and finish on the live-music strip in downtown Austin. You'll stop constantly for photos out in the flowers. Wineries, pink granite domes, and roadside blue stretching as far as you can possibly see. When are you booking your bluebonnet run this spring?
whyTrending: Texas Hill Country's bluebonnet bloom peaks in late March into April, blanketing roadsides and drawing wildflower-chasing crowds to Fredericksburg's wineries. Enchanted Rock and Hill Country swimming holes are spring viral favorites. It is one of the most-searched spring drives in the South.

title: West Glacier to St. Mary (Going-to-the-Sun Road)
subtitle: 50 miles by road
script: Fifty miles from West Glacier to St. Mary, and it costs you just 5 dollars in gas, but this road is only fully open about 10 weeks a year, and 2026's window is basically July through mid-September. Going-to-the-Sun Road climbs straight through the heart of Glacier National Park. You start at Lake McDonald with water so clear you can count the colored stones sitting on the bottom, switchback up to Logan Pass at 6,646 feet where mountain goats casually walk the parking lot, then drop past Rising Sun to the shore of St. Mary Lake. You'll need a timed-entry vehicle reservation, so book it the second they release. This is easily the most jaw-dropping stretch of pavement in the entire Rockies. Have you gotten your reservation yet?
whyTrending: Going-to-the-Sun Road is fully open only about 10 weeks a year, roughly July to mid-September, creating urgency and requiring timed-entry vehicle reservations. Glacier National Park's shrinking glaciers add cultural weight to seeing it now. It is a signature 2026 Rockies summer drive.

title: Jackson to Yellowstone (Grand Teton Loop)
subtitle: 120 miles by road
script: You could drive from Jackson all the way to West Yellowstone, 120 miles, on about 15 dollars of gas, crossing two of the greatest parks in the country. Old Faithful erupts roughly every 90 minutes, and this drive puts you right in front of it. Start in Jackson under its famous antler arches, roll into Grand Teton where the peaks shoot straight up 7,000 feet with no foothills, cruise past Jackson Lake, then enter Yellowstone for geysers, bison herds, and the Grand Prismatic Spring glowing rainbow colors. Late May and September dodge the worst summer crowds and the animals are most active. Two national parks back to back, all wildlife, all geothermal chaos. Which one would you road-trip first, Tetons or Yellowstone?
whyTrending: The Grand Teton to Yellowstone corridor is a top 2026 Rockies drive, with late May and September offering peak wildlife activity and thinner crowds than midsummer. Old Faithful and Grand Prismatic remain among the most filmed natural sights in America. Record park visitation is forecast for the 250th-anniversary travel year.

title: Chicago to Mackinac Island (Lake Michigan Shore)
subtitle: 400 miles by road
script: Did you know for about 50 dollars in gas you could drive 400 miles from Chicago to Mackinaw City? There's a sand dune on Lake Michigan you can climb that's 450 feet straight up, and the view at the top looks like the Caribbean. This Great Lakes run hits Milwaukee's breweries and lakefront, then crosses into Michigan for Sleeping Bear Dunes, the ones that beat out every beach for best in America. You stop in Traverse City, the cherry capital, then reach Mackinaw City where you ferry to Mackinac Island, an island with zero cars, only horses and bikes. Summer is prime, warm water and cherry season in July. Freshwater beaches that look tropical, who knew? Would you swap the ocean for the Great Lakes?
whyTrending: Lake Michigan's shoreline drive peaks in summer when Sleeping Bear Dunes, repeatedly named America's best beach, and Traverse City's July cherry season draw crowds. Car-free Mackinac Island is a perennial viral destination. Great Lakes road trips are a rising 2026 alternative to coastal drives.

title: Phoenix to Grand Canyon (Sedona Red Rocks)
subtitle: 230 miles by road
script: For about 30 dollars of gas, you can cross 230 miles from Phoenix to the Grand Canyon, going from cactus desert to snow-dusted pines to the edge of a mile-deep canyon in under four hours. From Phoenix you climb into Sedona, where the red rock formations glow so hard at sunset people literally gasp. You wind up Oak Creek Canyon, one of the twistiest scenic roads in Arizona, hit Flagstaff at 7,000 feet, then reach the South Rim, 277 miles long and a full mile deep. Spring and fall are ideal because the rim is freezing in winter and Phoenix is an oven in summer. One of the seven natural wonders of the world is at the finish line. Rate this route out of 10.
whyTrending: Sedona's red rocks and the Grand Canyon South Rim are ideal in spring and fall, avoiding Phoenix summer heat and winter rim freezes. Flagstaff's Route 66 centennial events add 2026 pull to this corridor. The Grand Canyon remains one of the most-visited and most-filmed U.S. natural wonders.

title: Denver to Aspen (Rocky Mountain Peaks)
subtitle: 160 miles by road
script: 160 miles from Denver to Aspen, and it costs you just 20 dollars in gas, but your car will feel every foot of altitude. Independence Pass tops out at 12,095 feet, one of the highest paved through-roads in America, and it's only open in summer. From Denver you climb west past the old silver town of Georgetown, hit Leadville, the highest incorporated city in the country at over 10,000 feet, then crawl up Independence Pass where the treeline vanishes and it's just tundra and sky. You drop into Aspen, ski-town glamour in the off-season. Late September torches the aspens gold across these valleys. This is Rocky Mountain driving at its most extreme. Could your car even make it up that pass?
whyTrending: Independence Pass, one of America's highest paved through-roads at over 12,000 feet, is only open in summer and closes with the first heavy snow. Late September lights the surrounding aspens gold, drawing peak leaf traffic to Aspen and Leadville. High-altitude Colorado byways are a marquee 2026 Rockies draw.

title: Nashville to Great Smoky Mountains
subtitle: 220 miles by road
script: You could drive from Nashville all the way to Gatlinburg, 220 miles, on about 30 dollars of gas, and end at the most-visited national park in America. Great Smoky Mountains pulls over 12 million people a year, more than double the Grand Canyon. Start with honky-tonks in Nashville, roll to Knoxville, then hit Pigeon Forge with its go-karts and Dollywood before entering the park. Cades Cove is the payoff, an 11-mile loop through a valley where black bears and deer graze in old homesteads. You finish in Gatlinburg at the park gateway. Mid-October sets these ridges on fire with color and the morning fog gives the Smokies their name. Why do you think this park beats every other one for visitors?
whyTrending: Great Smoky Mountains is the most-visited U.S. national park, and its mid-October fall color pulls record autumn crowds through Cades Cove and Gatlinburg. The famous morning fog and wildlife-rich valley loop are viral staples. Southeast leaf-peeping is a top 2026 fall search.

title: Portland to Crater Lake (Oregon Cascades)
subtitle: 250 miles by road
script: For about 30 dollars in gas, you can cross 250 miles from Portland to Crater Lake, the deepest lake in the United States at 1,949 feet, and so blue it doesn't look real. This drive earns it. From Portland you head to Mount Hood, an 11,000-foot volcano with a historic lodge, cut through the high desert to Bend, Oregon's outdoor playground with more breweries per capita than almost anywhere, cross Willamette Pass, and arrive at the caldera rim. The whole lake fills an old collapsed volcano with pure snowmelt. The rim road is only fully open July through October because of snow, so summer is your window. This blue has to be seen to be believed. Would you jump in that ice-cold water?
whyTrending: Crater Lake's rim drive is only fully open roughly July through October due to heavy snowpack, concentrating visits into the summer window. Bend and Mount Hood anchor a booming Oregon Cascades outdoor scene. It ranks among the most-searched Pacific Northwest 2026 summer drives.

title: Salt Lake City to Bryce Canyon (Utah Scenic Byway 12)
subtitle: 270 miles by road
script: Cross 270 miles from Salt Lake City to Bryce Canyon on about 35 dollars of gas, and you'll string together two of Utah's Mighty 5 on the way. Bryce Canyon has the largest collection of hoodoos on Earth, thousands of orange rock spires packed into one amphitheater. From Salt Lake City you pass Provo, reach Capitol Reef with its massive wrinkle of stone and free-picking orchards, then hit Scenic Byway 12, ranked one of the most beautiful roads in America, through Escalante's slot-canyon country. You finish at Bryce's rim looking down on a sea of hoodoos. Spring and fall dodge summer heat and winter snow up top. Two national parks, one legendary highway. How many hoodoos do you think you could count?
whyTrending: Utah Scenic Byway 12, an All-American Road, links Capitol Reef, Escalante's slot canyons, and Bryce Canyon's hoodoos as part of the Mighty 5 loop. Spring and fall are ideal between summer heat and winter snow. Utah's parks are seeing record 2026 interest tied to the anniversary travel year.

title: Taos to Red River (Enchanted Circle, New Mexico)
subtitle: 85 miles by road
script: Just 10 dollars in gas covers all 85 miles from Taos to Angel Fire, a tight loop that circles the highest peak in New Mexico, Wheeler Peak at 13,161 feet, and almost nobody outside the Southwest knows it exists. Start in Taos with its thousand-year-old adobe pueblo, still lived in today, drive north to Questa, hit the old mining-turned-ski town of Red River, pass the deep blue alpine lake at Eagle Nest, and finish up in Angel Fire. It's high desert crashing into real mountains. Late September and early October turn the aspens brilliant gold against the red mesas below. This is New Mexico's underrated Enchanted Circle Scenic Byway. Why do so few people know about this one?
whyTrending: New Mexico's Enchanted Circle Scenic Byway circles 13,161-foot Wheeler Peak and turns brilliant gold with aspens in late September and early October. Taos Pueblo and the alpine ski towns add year-round culture. It is a rising 2026 alternative to Colorado's busier fall byways.

title: Charleston to Savannah (Lowcountry Coast)
subtitle: 140 miles by road
script: Did you know for just 20 bucks of gas you could drive 140 miles from Charleston to Savannah? There's a stretch on this drive where 300-year-old oak trees form a tunnel dripping with Spanish moss, and it stops people cold. This Lowcountry run links two of the most beautiful historic cities in the South. Start in Charleston with its pastel Rainbow Row and clip-clopping horse carriages, detour out to quiet Edisto Island, roll through Beaufort where they filmed Forrest Gump, hit the wide beaches of Hilton Head, and finish under Savannah's oak-shaded historic squares. Spring is perfect, with the azaleas blooming and before the summer humidity gets absolutely brutal. Cobblestones, tidal marshes, and Spanish moss the whole way down. Charleston or Savannah, which city actually wins?
whyTrending: The Lowcountry drive between Charleston and Savannah peaks in spring when azaleas bloom and humidity stays low, spotlighting moss-draped oak avenues and historic squares. Both cities rank among the top-visited in the South. Coastal Southeast drives are a strong 2026 spring search.

title: Los Angeles to Grand Canyon (Route 66 Desert Leg)
subtitle: 380 miles by road
script: For about 50 dollars of gas you can cross 380 miles from Los Angeles to Williams, Arizona. There's a Route 66 town along the way where wild burros walk down Main Street and take snacks right out of your hand. With the road turning 100 in 2026, this desert leg is peak nostalgia. From LA you cross the Mojave to Barstow, hit Needles on the California line, then climb to Oatman where those burros roam free, roll into Kingman, the heart of historic 66, and finish in Williams, gateway to the Grand Canyon. Spring and fall keep the Mojave from cooking you. Vintage motels, neon, and burros begging for carrots the whole way through. Would you drive Route 66 in its centennial year?
whyTrending: This Mojave-to-Arizona stretch of Route 66 is prime for the 2026 centennial, with Oatman's wild burros and Kingman's historic district drawing centennial travelers. Spring and fall avoid brutal Mojave heat. Route 66's 100th birthday is the year's biggest American road-trip story.

title: Minneapolis to Duluth (Lake Superior North Shore)
subtitle: 260 miles by road
script: 260 miles from Minneapolis to Grand Marais, and it costs you just 35 dollars in gas. Lake Superior is the largest freshwater lake on Earth by surface area, so big it creates its own weather and looks like an ocean, and this drive traces Minnesota's North Shore along its edge. From Minneapolis you reach Duluth, a port city with lift bridges and ore boats, then follow Highway 61 past Two Harbors and the waterfalls at Gooseberry Falls, finishing in the artist town of Grand Marais. Late September and early October light up the birch and maple forests, and the lake stays a deep cold blue. Waterfalls, cliffs, and an inland sea. Have you ever seen a lake this big in person?
whyTrending: Minnesota's North Shore along Lake Superior, the largest freshwater lake on Earth by area, peaks for fall color in late September and early October. Duluth, Gooseberry Falls, and Grand Marais anchor the popular Lake Superior Circle Tour. Great Lakes fall drives are a growing 2026 draw.

title: Philadelphia to Boston (America 250 Freedom Trail)
subtitle: 350 miles by road
script: You could drive from Philadelphia all the way to Boston, 350 miles, on about 45 dollars of gas. In 2026 America turns 250 years old, and this route runs you straight through where the whole thing started. Begin in Philadelphia at Independence Hall where the Declaration was actually signed, visit Valley Forge where Washington's ragged army barely survived the winter, blow through the skyscrapers of New York City, detour to Newport's Gilded Age mansions on the Rhode Island coast, and finish walking Boston's brick-lined Freedom Trail. Every single one of these cities is throwing massive semiquincentennial events all year long. History, skylines, and ocean cliffs all in one shot. This is basically the founding story of the country on wheels. Where would you spend the Fourth of July in 2026?
whyTrending: July 4, 2026 marks America's 250th anniversary, and Philadelphia and Boston are hosting flagship semiquincentennial events at the nation's founding sites. Independence Hall, Valley Forge, and the Freedom Trail are central to the celebration year. It is a top patriotic 2026 travel theme.

title: San Diego to Joshua Tree (SoCal Desert)
subtitle: 180 miles by road
script: For about 25 dollars of gas you can cover 180 miles from San Diego to Joshua Tree. Joshua Tree only exists in one small pocket of the planet, and in spring the desert floor around it erupts in wildflowers. This drive climbs and drops through three landscapes. From San Diego you rise into Julian, a mountain town famous for its apple pie, plunge into Anza-Borrego, California's biggest state park where wildflower superblooms carpet the open sand, cruise into mid-century modern Palm Springs, and finish among the twisted Joshua trees themselves. Spring after a wet winter can trigger a full superbloom you'll never forget, and the clear desert nights bring some of the best stars in the state. Pie, palms, and alien-looking trees. Would you camp under those Joshua Tree stars?
whyTrending: Spring after a wet winter can trigger desert superblooms in Anza-Borrego and around Joshua Tree, drawing wildflower crowds to Southern California. Palm Springs and Julian anchor a popular seasonal loop. Desert superbloom chasing is a viral 2026 spring theme.

title: Rapid City to Devils Tower (Black Hills)
subtitle: 190 miles by road
script: 190 miles from Rapid City to Devils Tower, and it costs you just 25 dollars in gas. Mount Rushmore's faces are 60 feet tall, each one as high as a six-story building, and 2026 marks 250 years of the country they represent. This Black Hills drive is loaded. From Rapid City you reach Rushmore, drive the wildlife loop in Custer State Park where a herd of 1,500 bison roams free, wind through Spearfish Canyon's waterfalls, then cross into Wyoming for Devils Tower, the 867-foot rock column that starred in Close Encounters. Late spring and early fall dodge the summer motorcycle crowds. Presidents, bison, and a monolith that looks straight out of a movie. Which stop would you sprint to first?
whyTrending: The Black Hills draw patriotic 2026 interest to Mount Rushmore during America's 250th-anniversary year, paired with Custer State Park's free-roaming bison and Devils Tower. Late spring and early fall avoid the peak summer motorcycle-rally crowds. It is a top Great Plains bucket-list drive.

title: Burlington to Stowe (Vermont Green Mountains)
subtitle: 120 miles by road
script: Did you know for just 15 dollars in gas you could drive 120 miles from Burlington to Woodstock? Vermont peaks for fall color around the first week of October, earlier than almost anywhere else in the country, and this drive is postcard New England the whole way. Start in Burlington on the shore of Lake Champlain, climb to Stowe under Mount Mansfield, the state's highest peak, stop in Waterbury at the original Ben and Jerry's ice cream factory, roll through Montpelier, the tiniest state capital in all of America, and finish in Woodstock, routinely called the prettiest little town in the whole country. White church steeples, red barns, and old covered bridges buried in gold and crimson leaves. This is peak leaf-peeping season. Would you road-trip Vermont in October?
whyTrending: Vermont's Green Mountains hit peak fall foliage around the first week of October, among the earliest in New England, drawing leaf-peepers to Stowe and Woodstock. The 2026 forecast keeps the season roughly on schedule. Covered-bridge and steeple foliage shots are perennial viral fall content.

title: Tucson to Big Bend (Far West Texas)
subtitle: 480 miles by road
script: For about 60 dollars of gas you can cross 480 miles from Tucson to Big Bend. Big Bend has some of the darkest skies in the entire country, so dark you can see the Milky Way cast a shadow, and this desert crossing is a slow burn to get there. From Tucson you pass Las Cruces, hit El Paso on the border, then reach Marfa, the tiny art town famous for its mysterious unexplained lights, roll through Alpine, and finally enter Big Bend where the Rio Grande carves canyons and the Chisos Mountains rise out of nowhere. Spring and fall are the only sane seasons out here in the wide-open West Texas. Stars, ghost lights, and total silence. Could you handle a drive this remote?
whyTrending: Big Bend is a certified International Dark Sky Park with some of the darkest skies in the U.S., best experienced in the mild spring and fall seasons. Marfa's art scene and mystery lights add cult appeal. Remote West Texas dark-sky travel is a rising 2026 niche.

title: Atlanta to Blue Ridge (North Georgia Mountains)
subtitle: 130 miles by road
script: You could drive from Atlanta all the way to Clayton, 130 miles, on just 15 dollars of gas. There's a suspension bridge in North Georgia that hangs 80 feet over a gorge nearly 1,000 feet deep. This mountain escape from Atlanta packs it in. Start in the city, drive up to Dahlonega, site of America's first major gold rush back in 1828, roll into Helen, a fake Bavarian village that looks like it was teleported from Germany, then hit Tallulah Gorge with that swinging bridge, and finish near Clayton in the heart of the Blue Ridge. Mid to late October lights these foothills up and the summer river crowds are gone. Gold mines, German cottages, and a gorge you can dangle over. Would you cross that bridge?
whyTrending: North Georgia's Blue Ridge foothills hit fall color in mid-to-late October, drawing Atlanta day-trippers to Helen's Oktoberfest and Tallulah Gorge. Dahlonega's gold-rush heritage and wine country add year-round pull. It is a top accessible Southeast fall drive for 2026.

title: Reno to Yosemite (Lake Tahoe and the Sierra)
subtitle: 210 miles by road
script: For about 25 dollars of gas you can drive 210 miles from Reno to Yosemite. Lake Tahoe is so clear you can see objects 70 feet down, and this route runs right along its shore before climbing into Yosemite's high country. From Reno you drop to Tahoe's alpine blue, head south to Bridgeport, stop at eerie Mono Lake with its bizarre limestone towers rising from the water, then take Tioga Pass, the highest highway pass in California at 9,943 feet, into Tuolumne Meadows and down toward Yosemite Valley. Tioga Pass only opens roughly late May through October because of snow, so summer is your one shot. Crystal lakes, alien towers, and granite giants. Have you gotten your Yosemite reservation yet?
whyTrending: Tioga Pass, California's highest highway pass, opens only roughly late May through October, gating this Tahoe-to-Yosemite high-country route to summer. Lake Tahoe's clarity and Mono Lake's tufa towers are marquee stops. Yosemite peak-season reservations make it a high-demand 2026 drive.

title: Kansas City to St. Louis (Missouri Route 66 and BBQ)
subtitle: 250 miles by road
script: Did you know for about 30 dollars in gas you could drive 250 miles from Kansas City to St. Louis? Kansas City has over 100 barbecue joints, more per person than almost any city in America, and this drive starts there and ends at the Gateway Arch. With Route 66 turning 100 in 2026, Missouri is leaning hard into its stretch of the Mother Road. From KC you head to the college town of Columbia, stop in Fulton where Churchill gave his famous Iron Curtain speech, hit Rolla on historic 66, and finish under the 630-foot Gateway Arch, the tallest monument in the country. Fall means comfortable weather and centennial events all along the route. Burnt ends, history, and that giant arch. Which barbecue would you try first?
whyTrending: Missouri is spotlighting its Route 66 corridor for the 2026 centennial, and the Kansas City to St. Louis run pairs it with famous barbecue and the Gateway Arch. Fall brings comfortable weather and centennial programming. Route 66's 100th year drives strong Midwest interest.

title: Anchorage to Denali (Alaska Range)
subtitle: 240 miles by road
script: For about 30 dollars of gas you can cross 240 miles of pure Alaskan wilderness from Anchorage to Denali. Denali is the tallest peak in North America at 20,310 feet, but it's so massive it hides in the clouds about 70 percent of the time. This drive gives you the best shot at seeing it. From Anchorage you pass Wasilla, reach Talkeetna, the quirky town where climbers launch their summit attempts and the base for flightseeing, then roll through Cantwell to the gates of Denali National Park, with moose on the roadside the whole way. Summer, roughly June through August, is the only window with long daylight and the park road open. The mountain is out on maybe one in three days, so pray for a clear one. Would you risk it for the view?
whyTrending: The Anchorage-to-Denali corridor is Alaska's flagship summer drive, open with long daylight roughly June through August when the park road and Talkeetna flightseeing operate. Denali reveals its full summit only about a third of the time, adding chase appeal. Alaska ranks high among 2026 bucket-list road trips.

title: Louisville to the Smokies (Kentucky Bourbon Trail)
subtitle: 200 miles by road
script: 200 miles from Louisville to Cumberland Falls, and it costs you just 25 dollars in gas. Kentucky makes 95 percent of the world's bourbon, and this drive rolls right through the heart of it before ending at a waterfall that produces a moonbow. Start in Louisville, home of the Kentucky Derby, cruise to Bardstown, the bourbon capital with distilleries around every bend, pass through horse-country Lexington with its white-fenced farms, hit the artist town of Berea, and finish at Cumberland Falls, the only place in the Western Hemisphere where you can regularly see a moonbow on a full moon. Fall brings bourbon-barrel weather and gold hills. Distilleries, thoroughbreds, and a rainbow made of moonlight. Have you ever seen a moonbow?
whyTrending: The Kentucky Bourbon Trail draws heavy fall tourism as distilleries in Bardstown and Lexington run peak-season tours, and Cumberland Falls produces its famous moonbow on clear full-moon nights. Autumn pairs bourbon season with gold-hued hills. It is a distinctive 2026 Southeast drive.

title: Orlando to St. Augustine (Florida's First Coast)
subtitle: 110 miles by road
script: You could drive from Orlando all the way to St. Augustine, 110 miles, on just about 15 dollars of gas. St. Augustine is the oldest city in America, founded in 1565, more than 50 years before the Pilgrims even landed. This drive gets you there off the theme-park grid. Leave Orlando, hit Daytona Beach where you can actually drive your car on the hard-packed sand, cruise the mansion-lined loop at Ormond Beach, roll up the coast through Palm Coast, and finish in St. Augustine's cobblestone old town with its Spanish fort right on the water. Spring means warm beaches before the summer storms and humidity roll in. Beach driving, Gilded Age homes, and 460 years of history. Did you know America's oldest city was in Florida?
whyTrending: St. Augustine, the oldest continuously occupied European-founded city in the U.S., gains extra pull during the 2026 anniversary year of American history travel. Spring offers warm beaches before summer storms, with Daytona's drivable sand as a highlight. Florida First Coast drives are a strong spring search.

title: Cheyenne to Rocky Mountain National Park
subtitle: 170 miles by road
script: For about 20 dollars in gas you can drive 170 miles from Cheyenne to Estes Park. Trail Ridge Road is the highest continuous paved road in America, topping out at 12,183 feet where you're literally driving above the treeline in the alpine tundra. This route earns that summit. From Cheyenne you drop to Fort Collins, Colorado's craft-beer capital, then approach Rocky Mountain National Park from the quiet Grand Lake side, climb Trail Ridge Road across the roof of the Rockies with elk grazing and marmots whistling, and descend into Estes Park. The road only opens roughly late May through October before snow shuts it down, so summer and early fall are your window. Above the clouds, above the trees. Would your ears handle 12,000 feet?
whyTrending: Trail Ridge Road, the highest continuous paved road in the U.S., opens only roughly late May through October, concentrating Rocky Mountain National Park's alpine drive into summer and early fall. Elk rutting season in September adds a wildlife draw around Estes Park. It is a signature 2026 Rockies summit drive.

title: Banff to Jasper on the Icefields Parkway
subtitle: 144 miles by road
script: 144 miles from Banff to Jasper, and it costs you just 20 dollars in gas. There are 100 ancient glaciers you can see from that single road in Alberta, and most drivers blow past 90 of them. This is the Icefields Parkway. First stop, Lake Louise, that impossible turquoise you've seen a thousand times, except in person the color actually hurts your eyes. Keep north to Saskatchewan River Crossing where three valleys collide. Then the Columbia Icefield, where you can walk on a sheet of ice older than every pyramid on Earth. The Athabasca Glacier has retreated over a mile since 1890, and it shrinks a little more every summer you wait. You are literally watching a countdown. Would you drive it now, or gamble the ice is still there in ten years?
whyTrending: The Icefields Parkway is consistently ranked Canada's premier scenic drive and peaks in summer 2026 when mountain passes are fully clear. The Athabasca Glacier's visible, accelerating retreat has turned it into a bucket-list, see-it-before-it-changes destination. Jasper's post-2024-wildfire recovery has also drawn renewed traveler attention to the corridor.

title: Baddeck to Ingonish on the Cabot Trail
subtitle: 185 miles by road
script: You could drive from Baddeck all the way to Ingonish, 185 miles, on about 25 dollars of gas. Nine of the ten best ocean lookoffs in Atlantic Canada sit on that one loop, and it's not in the place you think. This is the Cabot Trail on Cape Breton Island. Start in Baddeck, then chase the west coast up to Cheticamp, where the road turns into a wall of Acadian fishing colour against the sea. Climb into Cape Breton Highlands National Park to Pleasant Bay, Canada's whale-watching capital, where pilot whales surface right off the cliffs. Round Cape North and the whole Atlantic opens beneath you before you drop into Ingonish. Do it the last week of September and every hillside is on fire with red and gold. Which side would you drive first, mountains or ocean?
whyTrending: The Cabot Trail is regularly named one of the world's best drives and hits peak demand during Cape Breton's Celtic Colours festival and fall foliage window in October 2026. The narrow two-week color peak drives urgency, and pilot-whale sightings in Pleasant Bay make late summer through fall the marquee season.

title: Vancouver to Whistler on the Sea-to-Sky Highway
subtitle: 75 miles by road
script: For about 10 dollars of gas you can cross 75 miles from Vancouver to Whistler, going from a downtown coffee to a glacier before lunch. This is Highway 99, the Sea-to-Sky. Roll out of Vancouver to Horseshoe Bay, where the fjord suddenly slams up against the road on your left. Push north to Squamish and look up, that granite monolith is the Stawamus Chief, one of the largest exposed rocks on the planet, with climbers dangling off it like ants. Stop at Brandywine Falls, a 230-foot ribbon dropping into a canyon. Then the mountains close in and spit you out in Whistler. Ride the Peak 2 Peak gondola across two summits in a glass floor cabin. It's paved, easy, and jaw-dropping the whole way. When are you finally booking it?
whyTrending: The Sea-to-Sky Highway is accessible year-round and surges every summer as Vancouver's easiest big-mountain escape, with Whistler's alpine season peaking in 2026. Squamish has boomed as a rock-climbing and outdoor-adventure hub, and the drive's short length makes it a favorite for short-form travel content.

title: Sainte-Flavie to Perce Around the Gaspe Peninsula
subtitle: 235 miles by road
script: Did you know for about 30 dollars in gas you could drive 235 miles from Sainte-Flavie to Perce? One rock in Quebec is 375 million years old and has a hole punched clean through it big enough to sail a boat into. To reach it you drive one of Canada's greatest coastal roads, Route 132 around the Gaspe Peninsula, pure St. Lawrence shoreline. Leave Sainte-Flavie and follow the water to Matane, salmon and lighthouse country. Continue to Sainte-Anne-des-Monts, gateway to the Chic-Choc mountains that plunge straight into the sea. Roll through Gaspe town, then the road bends and there it is, Perce Rock, an 85-metre limestone wall standing alone in the ocean. Just offshore, Bonaventure Island holds 110,000 nesting gannets, the world's most accessible colony. Ready to drive the edge of Quebec?
whyTrending: Perce Rock and Bonaventure Island's gannet colony have made the Gaspe a Michelin three-star and National Geographic-listed drive, with peak conditions from May to early September 2026. The region has surged as Quebecers and international visitors seek uncrowded maritime alternatives to overtouristed European coasts.

title: Deer Lake to St. Anthony on the Viking Trail
subtitle: 304 miles by road
script: 304 miles from Deer Lake to St. Anthony, and it costs you just 40 dollars in gas. Vikings landed in North America 500 years before Columbus was born, and there's exactly one place you can prove it. That's the finish line of this drive up Newfoundland's west coast, the Viking Trail. From Deer Lake head to Rocky Harbour, base camp for Gros Morne National Park, where a boat tour glides you through a landlocked fjord walled by 2,000-foot cliffs. Continue to Port au Choix and its ancient burial grounds, then push to L'Anse aux Meadows, the only confirmed Norse settlement in the Americas, a UNESCO site with costumed interpreters in real sod huts. In spring, 10,000-year-old icebergs drift past in Iceberg Alley, but that window slams shut by early July. Could you resist chasing a thousand-year-old story?
whyTrending: The Viking Trail links two UNESCO World Heritage Sites, Gros Morne and L'Anse aux Meadows, and its Iceberg Alley season peaks from late May to early July 2026. Newfoundland tourism has trended sharply as travelers seek raw, remote coastlines and the rare chance to see 10,000-year-old icebergs from land.

title: Dawson City to Inuvik on the Dempster Highway
subtitle: 458 miles by road
script: For about 60 dollars in gas you can drive 458 miles from Dawson City all the way to Inuvik, across the only public road in Canada that crosses the Arctic Circle. This is the Dempster Highway, 458 miles of gravel with no cell signal the entire way. Leave Dawson City and within two hours you hit Tombstone Territorial Park, jagged black granite peaks people call the Patagonia of the North. Push on to Eagle Plains, the only bed for hundreds of miles, then cross into the Northwest Territories at Fort McPherson before rolling into Inuvik. Drive it in late August and the whole tundra ignites crimson and gold to the horizon. In summer the sun never sets. Are you brave enough for the last frontier road?
whyTrending: The Dempster is Canada's only all-season public road across the Arctic Circle and a 2026 bucket-list release for adventure travelers, with dedicated new travel guides published for the route. Late-August tundra color and the midnight-sun driving window make summer 2026 the marquee season, while its remoteness fuels viral, off-grid appeal.

title: Kelowna to Osoyoos Through Okanagan Wine Country
subtitle: 80 miles by road
script: Just 10 bucks of gas gets you 80 miles from Kelowna to Osoyoos, past more than 150 wineries strung along a single lake. This is the Okanagan, and you can taste your way down the whole valley in one afternoon. Start in Kelowna's lakefront tasting rooms, then take the Naramata Bench, a cliffside ribbon of vineyards hanging right over the water. Roll into Penticton, wedged between two lakes with a beach on each end, then reach Oliver, the self-declared wine capital of Canada. Finish in Osoyoos, home to Canada's only true desert, where the beach hits 40 degrees in summer. Harvest season in September means crush festivals and grapes still on the vine. Which winery patio are you booking that lakefront table at first?
whyTrending: The Okanagan is Canada's premier wine region and peaks during the September 2026 harvest and crush festival season. Its lakefront winery restaurant scene in Kelowna, Penticton, and Oliver has surged as a food-and-wine destination, and Osoyoos's desert-beach climate draws summer travelers seeking a warm-weather Canadian escape.

title: Charlottetown to North Cape Across PEI
subtitle: 130 miles by road
script: 130 miles from Charlottetown to North Cape, and it costs you just 15 dollars in gas to cross an island whose beaches are the color of rust. Welcome to Prince Edward Island, where one tiny place grows a third of all the potatoes in Canada. Leave the pastel capital and head for Cavendish, the real-life home of Anne of Green Gables, with red cliffs dropping into white-sand dunes. Cut west to Summerside, then up the North Cape Coastal Drive through Tignish, oyster and lobster country where the wharves still smell of the morning catch. End at North Cape itself, where two tides literally crash into each other at the tip and a giant wind farm spins over the sea. The red sand comes from iron in the soil. Could you eat a whole lobster on every beach you pass?
whyTrending: PEI's coastal drives and red-sand beaches trend every summer, with Cavendish's Anne of Green Gables tourism peaking in the July-August 2026 season. The island's oyster and lobster food scene has grown as a culinary draw, and North Cape's colliding-tides phenomenon makes it a distinctive, shareable stop.

title: Sault Ste. Marie to Thunder Bay Along Lake Superior
subtitle: 435 miles by road
script: You could drive from Sault Ste. Marie all the way to Thunder Bay, 435 miles, on about 55 dollars of gas, hugging the wild northern shore of a lake so big it holds ten percent of all the fresh water on Earth. This is the Lake Superior stretch of the Trans-Canada. Roll out of Sault Ste. Marie and stop at the Agawa Rock pictographs, paintings left on the cliffs centuries ago. Reach Wawa and its 28-foot steel goose, then White River, the real birthplace of the bear who became Winnie the Pooh. Cruise the coast to Terrace Bay before finishing in Thunder Bay under the Sleeping Giant, a mesa shaped exactly like a reclining man. In fall the maple forests explode against that impossibly blue water. Could you handle hours with nothing but lake and forest?
whyTrending: The Lake Superior Circle Tour is billed as one of North America's most scenic road trips, and its Ontario shore peaks with fall foliage in late September and October 2026. Interest has climbed among travelers seeking uncrowded, remote Great Lakes routes, with cultural draws like the Agawa pictographs and the Winnie-the-Pooh origin story adding shareable stops.

title: Calgary to Waterton Along the Cowboy Trail
subtitle: 190 miles by road
script: For about 25 dollars in gas you can cross 190 miles from Calgary to Waterton along the exact line where the flat prairie smacks straight into the Rocky Mountains. This is Highway 22, the Cowboy Trail, and nowhere else on the continent is the wall this sharp. Head out of Calgary to Bragg Creek, where the foothills start rolling. Drop into Longview, real working-ranch country that doubled as the backdrop in the show Yellowstone. Continue to Pincher Creek, one of the windiest towns in Canada with turbines spinning to the horizon. Then finish in Waterton, where prairie grass and glacier peaks meet at a lake in the first International Peace Park on Earth. Watch for wild horses and pronghorn along the fence lines. Would you trade the crowds of Banff for real cowboy country?
whyTrending: The Cowboy Trail has surged as an uncrowded alternative to Banff, boosted by Alberta's on-screen appearances in ranch dramas. Waterton Lakes anchors the world's first International Peace Park, and its brief late-September golden-larch window plus fall ranch culture make autumn 2026 a peak draw.

title: Whitehorse to Skagway on the South Klondike Highway
subtitle: 110 miles by road
script: 110 miles from Whitehorse to Skagway, and it costs you just 15 dollars in gas to drive a route gold-rush stampeders once crossed hauling a literal ton of gear over a mountain pass on foot. This is the South Klondike Highway, and today you can do it in two hours flat. Start in the Yukon capital and reach Carcross, home to the smallest desert in the world at barely one square mile of sand dunes ringed by mountains. Swing by Tagish, then climb into British Columbia at Fraser as the trees vanish into raw alpine tundra. Crest White Pass and the road plunges 3,000 feet down to Skagway, Alaska, past waterfalls tumbling straight off the cliffs. You cross three borders in one afternoon. Which stampeder would you have been, brave or broke?
whyTrending: The South Klondike Highway retraces the 1898 Gold Rush route and peaks in summer 2026 when White Pass finally clears of snow. Interest has climbed with Yukon's rise as an off-the-beaten-path destination, and the surreal Carcross Desert plus a three-territory-and-state crossing in one day give it strong shareable hooks.

title: Moncton to Halifax Around the Bay of Fundy
subtitle: 170 miles by road
script: For about 20 dollars of gas you can drive 170 miles from Moncton to Halifax, chasing the highest tides on the planet. They rise 50 feet twice a day here, taller than a four-story building, and you can walk on the ocean floor hours before it vanishes underwater. Start at the Hopewell Rocks, giant flowerpot-shaped stone towers you stroll between at low tide, then paddle a kayak past their tops when the water floods back in. Continue to Alma, a fishing village with the freshest lobster rolls in New Brunswick, then cross into Nova Scotia at Truro to watch the tidal bore, a wave that literally reverses a river. Finish in the harbour city of Halifax. Time your visit to catch both low and high tide in one day. Could you handle standing where the sea disappears?
whyTrending: The Bay of Fundy's world-record 50-foot tides make it a marquee Atlantic Canada attraction, peaking in the warm May-to-September 2026 season for ocean-floor walks and kayaking. It anchors popular multi-province Maritime road-trip itineraries, and the walk-the-ocean-floor and tidal-bore phenomena are naturally viral, shareable experiences.

title: Inverness to Durness (North Coast 500 North Leg)
subtitle: 130 miles by road
script: You could drive 130 miles from Inverness to Durness on about 15 dollars of gas, and this northern leg is the part of the North Coast 500 that breaks the internet. You start in Inverness, capital of the Highlands, and follow the coast the locals actually drive. Stop in Dornoch, where the sand runs for three miles and the cathedral is 800 years old. Then Dunrobin Castle rises out of the trees like something from a fairy tale, all 189 rooms of it. Push north to Smoo Cave, a sea cavern the Vikings once sheltered in, with a waterfall crashing inside it. You finish in Durness, edge of the mainland, where the beaches are white and the water looks Caribbean until you touch it. Would you drive the wildest coast in Britain, or just keep watching people do it?
whyTrending: The North Coast 500 marks its tenth anniversary in 2026 and VisitScotland has been actively steering travellers to quieter shoulder-season stretches to ease summer congestion. The northern leg past Smoo Cave and Durness is the most photographed section on TikTok, and clearer spring light makes the white-sand beaches look tropical.

title: Applecross to Torridon (Bealach na Ba Pass)
subtitle: 42 miles by road
script: 42 miles from Applecross to Torridon, and it costs you just 5 dollars in gas to tackle Britain's steepest road. The Bealach na Ba, the Pass of the Cattle, throws you up 626 metres of climb in under six miles, a single-track wall of hairpins that Alpine drivers come here to test. From the top on a clear day you can see the Outer Hebrides. You drop into Applecross, a peninsula so remote that life genuinely slows down, then wind the coast to Shieldaig, a whitewashed fishing village on the water. The finale is Loch Torridon, ringed by 750-million-year-old mountains that go pink at sunset. Drive it early morning to beat the traffic and catch the golden light. Could you handle Britain's most dangerous road, or would you turn back at the first hairpin?
whyTrending: The Bealach na Ba Sportive returns in May 2026, putting the pass back in cycling and driving headlines. It is consistently ranked Britain's most dramatic road climb, and reels of the hairpin ascent have been racking up millions of views as NC500 traffic pushes drivers toward the wilder Applecross detour.

title: Glasgow to Fort William via Glencoe
subtitle: 104 miles by road
script: For about 15 dollars of gas you can drive 104 miles from Glasgow to Fort William, from Scotland's biggest city to its most cinematic glen in under three hours. Leave Glasgow and within 40 minutes you hit Loch Lomond, the largest lake in mainland Britain. Then the road empties out across Rannoch Moor, 50 square miles of peat bog and lochans with not a building in sight. And then Glencoe opens up, the valley that swallowed James Bond in Skyfall, walled by peaks that seem to lean over the road. You finish in Fort William, under Ben Nevis, the highest mountain in the UK at 1,345 metres. This is the drive every film director steals. When are you finally booking it?
whyTrending: Glencoe's Skyfall and Harry Potter viaduct connections keep it on 2026 must-drive lists, and the A82 stays open year-round. With Fort William hosting the UCI Mountain Bike World Cup again in 2026, the corridor from Glasgow is seeing renewed traffic from film-location and adventure travellers.

title: Portree to Neist Point (Isle of Skye Loop)
subtitle: 58 miles by road
script: Did you know for about 5 dollars in gas you could drive 58 miles from Portree all the way to Neist Point? There are 50 million years of geology packed into one island, and this loop hits the best of it. Start in Portree, Skye's pastel harbour town, then drive 20 minutes to the Old Man of Storr, a 50-metre rock spire that stands alone like a broken tooth on the ridge. Next comes the Quiraing, a landslip so strange it still moves a few centimetres every year. Swing south to the Fairy Pools, crystal blue plunge pools where people actually swim in the ice water. You finish at Neist Point lighthouse, the westernmost tip, best sunset on the island. Everyone's seen Skye on a screen. Ready to see it through a windscreen?
whyTrending: The Isle of Skye remains one of the most searched UK destinations for 2026, with the Highland Council introducing seasonal parking measures at the Fairy Pools and Storr to manage record visitor numbers. Spring drives avoid the summer crush and catch the clearest views of the Cuillin ridge.

title: Aviemore to Braemar (Cairngorms Crossing)
subtitle: 51 miles by road
script: For about 5 dollars of gas you can cross 51 miles from Aviemore to Braemar, over the highest main road in Britain. It climbs to 670 metres, and in winter it is genuinely a ski run away. Start in Aviemore, gateway to the Cairngorms, the UK's largest national park at 1,748 square miles. Drive to Loch Morlich, a mountain lake with a sandy beach 300 metres above sea level, backed by pine forest and reindeer. Then take the Glenshee pass over the top, past the ski slopes, before dropping toward Balmoral Castle, the royal family's Highland home for over 170 years. You finish in Braemar, one of the coldest villages in Britain, where the Highland Games still draw thousands. Snow, castles and reindeer in one drive. What are you waiting for?
whyTrending: The Cairngorms are Britain's premier snow-holiday region and the Braemar Gathering draws royal attendance each September, keeping 2026 interest high. Balmoral has expanded public tours in recent seasons following renewed royal visitor interest, driving searches for the surrounding Deeside drive.

title: Edinburgh to St Andrews (Fife Coast)
subtitle: 55 miles by road
script: 55 miles from Edinburgh to St Andrews, and it costs you just 5 dollars in gas. You trade a capital city for the home of golf, and cross one of the world's great bridges doing it. Leave Edinburgh and roll over the Forth, three bridges side by side, one of them 135 years old and a UNESCO site. Then follow the East Neuk of Fife, a string of fishing villages frozen in time. Stop in Anstruther for the best fish and chips in Scotland, they have the awards to prove it. Wander Crail, a harbour so pretty artists have painted it for centuries. You finish in St Andrews, where golf was born 600 years ago and the ruined cathedral still towers over the sea. A perfect day trip hiding an hour from the capital. Who are you taking?
whyTrending: St Andrews returns to the global spotlight as the Open Championship rotation and R&A anniversary events keep Fife in 2026 travel headlines. The East Neuk fishing villages have surged on food-travel content, and the Forth Bridge's UNESCO status continues to pull heritage drivers.

title: Kendal to Keswick (Lake District Passes)
subtitle: 40 miles by road
script: You could drive from Kendal all the way to Keswick, 40 miles, on about 5 dollars of gas. England's biggest national park fits into a single afternoon drive, and it inspired half of English poetry. Start in Kendal, the gateway town, then reach Windermere, the largest natural lake in England at 11 miles long. Push on to Ambleside, where stone cottages cluster at the head of the water and the fells rise straight up behind. Stop in Grasmere, the village Wordsworth called the loveliest spot man hath ever found, and buy the gingerbread they have made there since 1854. Then the road climbs over the passes toward Keswick, under the crags of Skiddaw and beside Derwentwater. This is the landscape that invented the scenic view. Have you actually driven it, or just seen the postcards?
whyTrending: The Lake District, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, tops 2026 spring self-drive lists as travellers seek early-season quiet before summer congestion. The Wordsworth Grasmere connection continues to fuel literary tourism, and daffodil season in April makes the fells especially photogenic.

title: Chipping Campden to Bourton-on-the-Water (Cotswolds)
subtitle: 22 miles by road
script: For just 5 bucks of gas you can drive 22 miles from Chipping Campden to Bourton-on-the-Water, linking four of the prettiest villages in England. Start in Chipping Campden, where a honey-coloured High Street has barely changed in 400 years. Roll down to Broadway, so elegant they call it the jewel of the Cotswolds. Climb to Stow-on-the-Wold, the highest of the wool towns, with a church door flanked by 1,000-year-old yew trees. Slip into Lower Slaughter, where a clear stream runs right past the cottage gardens and a working mill still turns. You finish in Bourton-on-the-Water, the Venice of the Cotswolds, low bridges crossing the river through the middle of town. It is impossibly pretty. When are you going before it gets packed?
whyTrending: The Cotswolds exploded in 2025-26 as an American celebrity-driven destination, with a surge in international searches and local councils warning of overtourism in villages like Bourton-on-the-Water. Spring is pushed heavily as the season to visit before summer coach crowds arrive.

title: Swanage to Lyme Regis (Jurassic Coast)
subtitle: 40 miles by road
script: 40 miles from Swanage to Lyme Regis, and it costs you just 5 dollars in gas. That drive exposes 185 million years of Earth's history, England's only natural World Heritage Site. Start in Swanage, then walk out to Old Harry Rocks, chalk stacks marking where this coast once joined France. Drive west to Durdle Door, a limestone arch so perfect it looks designed, arguably the most photographed rock in Britain. Carry on to West Bay, the golden sandstone cliffs you know from the show Broadchurch. You finish in Lyme Regis, where a 12-year-old girl named Mary Anning dug up the first ichthyosaur skeleton in 1811 and rewrote science. People still find fossils on the beach here every single week. Would you rather find a dinosaur bone or the perfect sunset shot?
whyTrending: The Jurassic Coast continues to ride the fossil-hunting wave sparked by renewed interest in Mary Anning, whose story reached new audiences through film and museum exhibitions. Durdle Door is one of Instagram's most-tagged UK landmarks, and spring drives dodge the summer coastal congestion.

title: Barnstaple to Tintagel (Atlantic Highway)
subtitle: 70 miles by road
script: Did you know for about 10 dollars in gas you could drive 70 miles from Barnstaple to Tintagel? That whole run hugs the wildest coast in England along the A39. Start in Barnstaple, then drop into Clovelly, a village so steep cars are banned and goods still come down by sledge. Push on to Hartland Quay, where the cliffs are folded and twisted like something molten, some of the most dramatic geology in Britain. Cross into Cornwall at Bude, all surf beaches and sea pools carved into the rock. You finish at Tintagel, the clifftop castle where legend says King Arthur was born, now crossed by a footbridge that hangs over the sea. Storms, surf and Camelot in one drive. Could you handle the coast the Atlantic beats up every winter?
whyTrending: The A39 Atlantic Highway is featured on multiple 2026 best-coastal-drive lists, and Tintagel's cliff bridge continues to draw Arthurian-legend tourists. Cornwall's surf scene keeps Bude trending on travel content, and off-peak spring driving avoids the notorious summer congestion on the narrow coastal roads.

title: Bakewell to Castleton (Peak District)
subtitle: 20 miles by road
script: For about 5 dollars of gas you can cross 20 miles from Bakewell to Castleton, deep into the Peak District. 13 million people live within an hour of this national park, yet the drive still feels like escaping the planet. Start in Bakewell, home of the famous tart, then visit Chatsworth House, a 297-room palace that has stood for 470 years and starred as Pemberley in Pride and Prejudice. Then comes Winnats Pass, a limestone gorge so narrow the road threads between 60-metre cliffs. Above it looms Mam Tor, the shivering mountain, where you can walk the ridge for views over two valleys at once. You finish in Castleton, sitting on top of caverns full of Blue John, a purple stone found nowhere else on Earth. Britain's first national park is still its best kept secret. When do you go?
whyTrending: The Peak District, Britain's first national park, celebrates ongoing anniversary programming and Winnats Pass has become one of the most viral driving clips on UK TikTok. Chatsworth's period-drama fame keeps it a top 2026 heritage draw, and spring brings the clearest ridge-walk visibility on Mam Tor.

title: Skipton to Hawes (Yorkshire Dales)
subtitle: 35 miles by road
script: 35 miles from Skipton to Hawes, and it costs you just 5 dollars in gas. One cliff on the way is 80 metres of sheer limestone, and it doubled as a Harry Potter filming location. Start in Skipton, the gateway to the Dales, then drive to Malham Cove, a curved wall of white rock where Harry and Hermione camped on screen, with a rare limestone pavement across the top. Follow the B6160 up Wharfedale through Kettlewell, a village of stone cottages and dry stone walls that climb the fells. Then swing to Aysgarth Falls, three tiers of waterfall where Robin Hood fought Little John in the Kevin Costner film. You finish in Hawes, home of real Wensleydale cheese, the stuff Wallace and Gromit made famous. Waterfalls and film history in one drive. Which stop are you filming first?
whyTrending: The Yorkshire Dales keeps drawing screen-tourism crowds thanks to its Harry Potter and Robin Hood filming sites, and the B6160 through Wharfedale is repeatedly named one of northern England's finest drives. All Creatures Great and Small's continued success has kept the Dales trending into 2026.

title: Alnwick to Lindisfarne (Northumberland Coast)
subtitle: 30 miles by road
script: You could drive from Alnwick all the way to Lindisfarne, 30 miles, on about 5 dollars of gas. One castle on this drive played Hogwarts in two Harry Potter films, and it is only the warm-up act. Start in Alnwick, then tour Alnwick Castle, 950 years old, where the young wizards first learned to fly a broomstick. Drive up the coast to Craster, a tiny harbour famous for oak-smoked kippers since the 1800s. Then Bamburgh Castle appears, nine acres of fortress on a dune above the beach, one of the most dramatic buildings in England. You finish at Lindisfarne, the Holy Island, reached across a causeway that vanishes under the tide twice a day, where English Christianity took root 1,400 years ago. Castles, kippers and a road the sea swallows. Would you time the tide right, or get stranded?
whyTrending: Northumberland's coast, an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty, has surged as a quieter alternative to crowded southern beaches, and Alnwick Castle's Harry Potter fame keeps it a 2026 family-travel magnet. The tidal Lindisfarne causeway remains one of the UK's most shared and cautionary drive clips.

title: Betws-y-Coed to Llanberis (Eryri / Snowdonia)
subtitle: 22 miles by road
script: 22 miles from Betws-y-Coed to Llanberis, and it costs you just 5 dollars in gas, past the highest mountain in Wales. Start in Betws-y-Coed, the stone-built gateway to Eryri, then stop at Swallow Falls, where the river Llugwy tumbles through the forest in white terraces. Climb to Pen-y-Pass, the high saddle beneath Yr Wyddfa, Snowdon, the tallest peak in Wales and England at 1,085 metres, where the classic climbs begin. Then drop toward Llyn Padarn, a glacial lake that mirrors the mountains on a still morning. You finish in Llanberis, where a Victorian railway has hauled visitors to the summit since 1896. Peaks, waterfalls and a lake in half an hour. Would you climb Snowdon or just drive around it?
whyTrending: Eryri (Snowdonia) National Park introduced booking systems for its most popular trailheads to manage record 2025-26 visitor numbers, pushing drivers toward the scenic loop. The Welsh-language renaming of Yr Wyddfa and Eryri has driven a wave of cultural-tourism content into 2026.

title: Cardiff to Tenby (Pembrokeshire Coast)
subtitle: 100 miles by road
script: Did you know for about 15 dollars in gas you could drive 100 miles from Cardiff to Tenby, to a beach voted the best in Britain more than once? Leave Cardiff, the Welsh capital, and follow the coast to Swansea, then out onto the Gower, the first place in Britain ever named an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. Stop at Rhossili Bay, three miles of golden sand under a green headland, regularly ranked among the best beaches in the world. Wind on to Saundersfoot, a pretty harbour town on the edge of the only coastal national park in the UK. You finish in Tenby, where pastel houses stack above the harbour inside 13th-century town walls. This stretch of Wales looks Mediterranean when the sun comes out. Ready to prove the UK has beaches like these?
whyTrending: The Pembrokeshire Coast is the UK's only coastal national park and Rhossili Bay repeatedly tops best-beach rankings, keeping the Gower and West Wales high on 2026 lists. The Wales Coast Path anniversary and rising interest in UK beach holidays over overseas travel have fuelled the surge.

title: Brecon to Abergavenny (Bannau Brycheiniog)
subtitle: 30 miles by road
script: 30 miles from Brecon to Abergavenny, and it costs you just 5 dollars in gas, looping the highest point in southern Britain. That summit is 886 metres, and around 600,000 people walk it every year, but the drive around it is the real secret. Start in Brecon, the market town at the heart of Bannau Brycheiniog, then pass beneath Pen y Fan, that flat-topped peak with the most dramatic north-facing cliffs in the country. Roll down to Talybont Reservoir, a strip of blue water ringed by forest and moor, a classic Welsh Sunday drive. Detour to Llangorse Lake, the largest natural lake in South Wales, where an ancient crannog island still sits offshore. You finish in Abergavenny, the gateway to the Beacons and a genuine foodie town. This is Wales at its most cinematic and least crowded. When are you swapping the gym for the mountains?
whyTrending: Bannau Brycheiniog (Brecon Beacons) earned International Dark Sky Reserve recognition and its 2023 Welsh-name rebrand keeps driving cultural interest into 2026. Pen y Fan draws around 600,000 walkers a year, and the Abergavenny Food Festival keeps the eastern gateway trending each autumn.

title: Llandudno to Portmeirion (North Wales Coast)
subtitle: 40 miles by road
script: You could drive from Llandudno all the way to Portmeirion — 40 miles — on about 5 dollars of gas, past a castle 740 years old. Start in Llandudno, the grand Victorian seaside resort under the Great Orme headland, then reach Conwy Castle, a UNESCO fortress wrapped by a walled town you can walk right around. Drive the coast to Caernarfon, where the mightiest of Edward the First's castles guards the Menai Strait and where Welsh princes were crowned. Roll on to Porthmadog, a harbour town where steam trains still run into the mountains. You finish at Portmeirion, an Italian fantasy village on the Welsh coast, built by one eccentric architect over 50 years. Castles, steam trains and a slice of Italy. Which stop breaks your feed first?
whyTrending: North Wales's Edwardian castles form a UNESCO World Heritage cluster that anchors 2026 heritage itineraries, and Portmeirion's Italianate village remains one of the most photographed spots in Wales. The area's steam-railway revival and Eryri gateway status keep it high on Welsh road-trip lists.

title: Inverness to Loch Ness and Eilean Donan
subtitle: 78 miles by road
script: For about 10 dollars of gas you can cross 78 miles from Inverness to Eilean Donan, along the most famous lake on Earth. Loch Ness holds more fresh water than every lake in England and Wales combined, and this drive runs right along it. Start in Inverness, then follow the shore of the loch, 23 miles long and 230 metres deep, still hunted for its legendary monster. Stop at Urquhart Castle, a ruined stronghold on a headland that has watched the water for 1,000 years, and the best monster-spotting perch on the loch. Drive west through Glen Shiel, past the Five Sisters, a wall of peaks that lines the road like a mountain range should. You finish at Eilean Donan, the castle on its own tiny island where three lochs meet, quite possibly the most photographed castle in Scotland. Would you go for the monster or the castle?
whyTrending: Loch Ness remains one of the most searched destinations on the planet, and 2026 marks continued interest following high-profile monster-hunting expeditions. Eilean Donan Castle is Scotland's most photographed castle and a fixture on Highlands driving itineraries, with the A87 through Glen Shiel a gateway to Skye.

title: Killarney to Killarney (Ring of Kerry Loop)
subtitle: 111 miles by road
script: 111 miles from Killarney back to Killarney, and it costs you just 15 dollars in gas. The Ring of Kerry starts and ends in Killarney, and most drivers rush it in a day when it deserves three. Leave Killarney early and go anti-clockwise so you beat the tour coaches locked into one direction. Stop in Killorglin for the puck goat statue, then Cahersiveen for the old barracks. Sneem splits into two tiny squares painted every colour Ireland owns. Kenmare is where you eat before the Ladies View climb back over the mountains. Every layby hides a photo, and no two views repeat, so which one makes your camera roll first?
whyTrending: As of April 2026 the Ring of Kerry is being named one of Europe's premier scenic drives, pulling transatlantic travellers to its Atlantic cliffs and monastic sites. Kerry's 2026 tourism outlook is pushing shoulder-season visits, so early-summer loops are trending as a way to dodge peak crowds.

title: Dingle to Dingle (Slea Head Drive)
subtitle: 30 miles by road
script: For about 5 dollars of gas you can loop 30 miles out of Dingle and back on the Slea Head Drive, a road Star Wars flew across the planet to film. It's strictly one-way clockwise, so start through Ventry or you'll fight oncoming traffic on cliff lanes. Ventry gives you the first gold beach, then Dunquin drops you at the pier that stares straight at the Blasket Islands. Ballyferriter is the Irish-speaking village where the road signs stop pretending to be English. You'll pass beehive stone huts a thousand years old and the Sybil Head ridge from The Last Jedi. Nobody does this loop just once. Two hours minimum, so are you rushing it or feeling it?
whyTrending: Slea Head Drive keeps climbing 2026 scenic-drive lists on the back of its Star Wars: The Last Jedi film locations at Sybil Head. Its one-way clockwise system for visitors is a 2026 talking point, and the Dingle Peninsula's Blasket Island views anchor most Wild Atlantic Way itineraries this year.

title: Belfast to Derry (Causeway Coastal Route)
subtitle: 115 miles by road
script: 115 miles from Belfast to Derry, and it costs you just 15 dollars in gas, along a coast rated among the best drives on Earth. The Causeway Coastal Route runs Belfast to Derry, so point north and let the Glens of Antrim open up. Carnlough's little harbour doubled as a Game of Thrones set. Ballycastle is your ferry town and your fish-and-chips stop before the big one. The Giant's Causeway stacks 40,000 basalt columns into the sea like nature ran out of ideas for anything round. Portrush closes it with beaches and dunes before Derry's walls. Three to five days does it justice, so who's driving?
whyTrending: The Causeway Coastal Route is repeatedly ranked among the world's most scenic drives, and 2026 guides push May-June for mild weather and long daylight. Game of Thrones filming locations like Carnlough harbour keep drawing screen-tourism fans, and the Giant's Causeway remains Northern Ireland's flagship attraction.

title: Galway to Clifden (Connemara)
subtitle: 50 miles by road
script: Did you know for about 5 dollars of gas you could drive 50 miles from Galway to Clifden, through a national park, a fjord and a castle? Leave Galway west and Connemara turns the greens darker with every mile toward Clifden. Oughterard sits on Lough Corrib, the gateway lake before the bog roads begin. Maam Cross is the crossroads where the Twelve Bens mountains finally fill your windscreen. Recess drops you beside Derryclare Lough, the mirror lake every photographer stops for. Then Kylemore Abbey rises out of the water like someone dropped a Victorian castle into a postcard. Signal drops out here, so download your map first. This is Ireland with the volume turned down, so would you go slow or slower?
whyTrending: Connemara National Park, the Twelve Bens and Kylemore Abbey headline the western stages of the Wild Atlantic Way in 2026 itineraries. The 2026 travel shift toward slower, more immersive trips suits Connemara's bog roads and mirror lakes, which are trending as a quieter alternative to the busier Ring of Kerry.

title: Doolin to Kinvara (Burren & Cliffs of Moher)
subtitle: 38 miles by road
script: For about 5 dollars of gas you can cross 38 miles from Doolin to Kinvara, past 700 feet of vertical cliff and a landscape NASA compared to the moon. Start in Doolin, the trad-music village, and drive straight to the Cliffs of Moher where the Atlantic hammers a wall taller than most skyscrapers. Lisdoonvarna is the spa town famous for Europe's biggest matchmaking festival every autumn. Then the Burren takes over, grey limestone pavement cracked with wildflowers that grow nowhere else together. Ballyvaughan sits on the bay before you finish in Kinvara under Dunguaire Castle. It looks barren until you stop and look down, so are you staying on the road or getting out?
whyTrending: The Burren Coastal Road is highlighted in 2026 as one of Ireland's most unique drives for its karst landscape meeting Atlantic views. The Cliffs of Moher stay a Wild Atlantic Way headline attraction, and Lisdoonvarna's long-running matchmaking festival keeps the town on autumn travel radars.

title: Kenmare to Glengarriff (Ring of Beara)
subtitle: 85 miles by road
script: For about 10 dollars in gas you can drive 85 miles from Kenmare to Glengarriff, and skip the coach-packed Ring of Kerry entirely. The Ring of Beara gives you the same Atlantic drama with a fraction of the traffic. From Kenmare, Lauragh eases you in with a sub-tropical garden nobody expects this far north. Allihies is the old copper-mining village where the houses are painted so bright they glow against the grey. Castletownbere is Ireland's biggest whitefish port, so lunch is off the boat that morning. Then Glengarriff and its harbour islands finish it. This is the loop locals actually recommend, so are you chasing the crowds or leaving them behind?
whyTrending: The Ring of Beara is flagged in 2026 scenic-drive guides as a coastal road with endless photo stops and far fewer crowds than the Ring of Kerry.
