The 2026 trend toward flexible, quieter shoulder-season driving makes Beara a rising pick for travellers wanting the Wild Atlantic Way without the tour buses.

title: Sligo to Donegal Town (Wild Atlantic North)
subtitle: 45 miles by road
script: Just 5 bucks of gas gets you 45 miles from Sligo to Donegal Town, along the wildest edge of the Atlantic, and most tourists drive straight past it. Ireland's biggest wave breaks right here. Start under Benbulben, the flat-topped mountain that looks fake until you see it. Mullaghmore is where big-wave surfers chase 50-foot swells every winter storm. Bundoran is the surf capital where beginners actually get in the water. Rossnowlagh gives you two full miles of golden beach you can drive your car straight onto. This entire corner gets skipped for the south, which is exactly why it's still empty and raw, so would you surf it or just watch?
whyTrending: The northern Wild Atlantic Way through Sligo and Donegal is gaining 2026 attention as the under-visited alternative to the crowded southwest. Mullaghmore's winter big-wave surf and Bundoran's surf-town reputation keep the stretch trending with adventure travellers, while Benbulben and Yeats country draw literary tourism.

title: Cork to Kinsale (Rebel Coast to the Head of Ireland)
subtitle: 55 miles by road
script: 55 miles from Cork to Kinsale, and it costs you just 5 dollars in gas. The last port the Titanic ever touched sits 20 minutes from where this drive starts. From Cork city, loop the harbour toward Kinsale, the town that officially ends the Wild Atlantic Way. Cobh is the deepwater port where 123 passengers boarded the Titanic in 1912, its cathedral towering over pastel row houses. Midleton is the home of Jameson, so the distillery tour is basically compulsory. Ballycotton hands you a cliff walk and a lighthouse on its own island. Then Kinsale, Ireland's gourmet capital, closes it with harbour restaurants. History, whiskey and seafood in one afternoon, so which stop are you skipping? None?
whyTrending: Kinsale is the official southern gateway of the Wild Atlantic Way, keeping it central to 2026 route planning. Cobh's Titanic heritage draws steady history tourism, and County Cork's food scene, anchored by Kinsale's gourmet reputation and the Jameson distillery at Midleton, feeds the 2026 appetite for immersive local experiences.

title: Melbourne to Port Campbell via the Great Ocean Road
subtitle: 150 miles by road
script: You could drive from Melbourne all the way to Port Campbell, 150 miles of coast on one road, for about 20 dollars of gas, a tank most people burn in a single Saturday. You leave Melbourne, hit Torquay where Australian surfing was basically invented, then the tarmac starts hugging cliffs above the Southern Ocean. Lorne is your first coffee stop, koalas sleep in the gums past Apollo Bay, and then the road bends inland through the Otway rainforest before spitting you back out at the Twelve Apostles. These limestone stacks are literally collapsing into the sea, one every few decades. Late summer is the sweet spot here, warm water, settled weather, golden light at sunset. Would you drive it end to end, or stop at every single lookout like the rest of us?
whyTrending: The Great Ocean Road peaks in late summer (March-April) when the water is still warm and the weather settles after the January crowds. The Twelve Apostles are visibly eroding, adding a see-it-before-it's-gone urgency that drives social shares. It consistently tops 2026 Australian road-trip guides.

title: Sydney to Wollongong on the Grand Pacific Drive
subtitle: 56 miles by road
script: Did you know for about 5 dollars in gas you could drive 56 miles from Sydney to Wollongong, across a bridge that hangs over the crashing ocean on stilts? This is the Grand Pacific Drive, and it starts the second you clear the Royal National Park, Australia's oldest. At Stanwell Tops, hang-gliders launch off the escarpment straight over your windscreen. Then comes the Sea Cliff Bridge, 665 metres of curved concrete that levitates above the waves because the cliff kept collapsing onto the old road. It's short, barely 90 kilometres, but every turn frames a new stretch of blue. Autumn gives you clear skies and no summer traffic. Have you crossed a bridge that floats over the sea before?
whyTrending: The Sea Cliff Bridge is one of the most photographed structures in NSW and a reliable viral hit. Autumn (March-May) brings clear skies and thins the summer crowds south of Sydney, making it a top short-drive pick in 2026 guides for people who only have a day.

title: Cairns to Cape Tribulation on the Great Barrier Reef Drive
subtitle: 87 miles by road
script: For about 10 dollars of gas you can cross 87 miles from Cairns to Cape Tribulation, the only road on Earth that runs between two World Heritage sites at once. You leave Cairns with the Great Barrier Reef on your right and the oldest rainforest on the planet on your left, closing in as you drive. Palm Cove is your last flat-white before the palms take over. Port Douglas is where reef boats leave every morning. Then you cross the Daintree River on a cable ferry into 180-million-year-old jungle where cassowaries actually cross the road. Cape Tribulation is where rainforest meets reef on the same beach, the only place that happens. Go May to October, dry season, no stingers, clear water. Where else can you snorkel a reef and hike a jungle in one afternoon?
whyTrending: The dry season (May-October) is peak for Far North Queensland: clear reef visibility, no box-jellyfish stinger risk, and hot sunny days. The Daintree is marketed as the oldest rainforest on Earth meeting the reef, a rare combination that dominates 2026 QLD itineraries.

title: Alice Springs to Uluru on the Red Centre Way
subtitle: 458 miles by road
script: 458 miles from Alice Springs to Uluru, and it costs you just 60 dollars in gas, or you can spend a lifetime wishing you had made the drive. The Red Centre Way heads straight into the West MacDonnell Ranges, where you swim in ice-cold gorges surrounded by red rock. Kings Canyon comes next, 100-metre sandstone walls you walk along the rim of at sunrise. Then Kata Tjuta, 36 domes older than the Alps. And finally Uluru, glowing like a coal at sunset in the middle of nothing. Winter, June to August, is the only sane time, warm days, freezing nights, no flies. This is the spiritual heart of the continent. Could you stand in front of Uluru and not go quiet?
whyTrending: Winter (June-August) is the only comfortable season in the Red Centre, with warm days and cold nights that keep the flies down. Uluru and Kata Tjuta remain Australia's most iconic cultural landmarks, and Kings Canyon rim walks trend heavily on travel feeds during the 2026 dry season.

title: Derby to Kununurra on the Gibb River Road
subtitle: 410 miles by road
script: You could cross 410 miles from Derby to Kununurra on about 50 dollars of gas, but the Gibb River Road makes you earn every one, red dirt and zero petrol stations for stretches of 300 kilometres. This is the outback drive that separates the tourists from the travellers. You leave Derby and hit Windjana Gorge, where freshwater crocodiles bask on the sand by the dozen. Bell Gorge has a waterfall you swim under. El Questro is a million-acre cattle station with thermal springs hidden in the ranges. This road only opens in the dry season, May to September, the rest of the year it's underwater. You need a 4WD, two spare tyres, and nerve. Would you take on Australia's most legendary outback road?
whyTrending: The Gibb River Road only opens in the dry season (May-September); the rest of the year the Kimberley floods. Early season delivers fuller waterfalls and greener gorges, and the 4WD-only challenge gives it cult status among adventure travellers filling out 2026 bucket lists.

title: Hobart to Bay of Fires on Tasmania's Great Eastern Drive
subtitle: 195 miles by road
script: For about 25 dollars of gas you can cross 195 miles from Hobart to the Bay of Fires, beaches so white the sand squeaks, orange lichen boulders, a whole coastline in a single day. That's Tasmania's Great Eastern Drive. You start in Hobart, detour to Port Arthur where the convict ruins sit on a haunting green peninsula, then head north. Freycinet hides Wineglass Bay, that perfect crescent you've seen a thousand times but never in person. Bicheno has a nightly penguin parade waddling up the beach. And then the Bay of Fires, where fire-orange rocks meet turquoise water under an empty sky. Autumn brings crisp air and thin crowds. Tasmania feels like a different country down here. Ready to trade the mainland for this?
whyTrending: Tasmania's Great Eastern Drive links Wineglass Bay and the Bay of Fires, two of the most-shared beach scenes in Australia. Autumn (March-May) delivers crisp, clear weather and far fewer people than the mainland summer, making it a 2026 favourite for slow-travel content.

title: Adelaide to Head of Bight on the Eyre Peninsula whale run
subtitle: 490 miles by road
script: 490 miles from Adelaide to the Head of Bight, and it costs you just 65 dollars in gas, enough to watch 100 southern right whales calving beneath a cliff, no boat required. This is the Eyre Peninsula run. You leave Adelaide and drive to Port Lincoln, the seafood capital where you can cage-dive with great whites. Coffin Bay is where you buy a dozen oysters straight off the boat and shuck them on the beach. Ceduna is the last real town before the Nullarbor swallows the map. Then Head of Bight, where the whale-watching centre opens June the first and mothers nurse their calves fifty metres below the boardwalk. Peak season runs June to September. This is raw, empty, edge-of-the-continent Australia. When did you last watch a whale from dry land?
whyTrending: The Head of Bight whale-watching centre opens 1 June 2026, and southern right whales calve below the cliffs with peak sightings June-September. Coffin Bay oysters and Port Lincoln shark diving make the Eyre Peninsula a rising winter road-trip destination in SA guides.

title: Sydney to Byron Bay on the Legendary Pacific Coast
subtitle: 620 miles by road
script: For about 80 dollars in gas you can drive 620 miles from Sydney all the way to Byron Bay, but nobody does this drive to reach Brisbane fast. The Legendary Pacific Coast starts in Sydney and swings inland to the Hunter Valley, the country's oldest wine region, cellar doors everywhere. Then Port Stephens, where sand dunes tower forty metres above the sea and you can toboggan down them. Coffs Harbour has the giant banana and dolphins offshore. And Byron Bay is the finish line, Australia's easternmost point, lighthouse on the cape, whales passing by in winter. It's a slow burn of beaches and wine over four days. Spring is glorious up here. Could you actually resist stopping in every single beach town?
whyTrending: The 1,000km Legendary Pacific Coast is Australia's classic Sydney-to-Brisbane run, and Byron Bay's whale migration peaks through the cooler months. Spring brings warm, settled weather along the NSW coast, and the route is heavily featured in 2026/2027 touring itineraries.

title: Perth to Esperance on the South West coast
subtitle: 450 miles by road
script: The whitest beach on Earth sits 450 miles from Perth, and getting there costs just about 60 bucks of gas. This is Western Australia's south west, and it's absurdly underrated. You leave Perth for Margaret River, world-class surf breaks and 200 wineries in one valley. Albany was WA's first settlement and sits on a wild granite coast where whales pass close to shore. Then you push east to Cape Le Grand, where kangaroos actually lounge on Lucky Bay, officially measured as Australia's whitest sand. Esperance is the frontier town at the end, ringed by beaches so blue they look edited. Spring wildflowers carpet the whole south west from September. This corner of Australia has no crowds and no equal. Would you drive four days just for one beach?
whyTrending: WA's south west explodes with wildflowers from September, one of the largest wildflower displays on Earth. Lucky Bay at Cape Le Grand is repeatedly ranked Australia's whitest beach and its lounging kangaroos are a viral staple, keeping this drive high on 2026 lists.

title: Brisbane to Noosa on the Sunshine Coast
subtitle: 87 miles by road
script: 87 miles from Brisbane to Noosa, and it costs you just 10 dollars in gas to reach one of the best beach towns in the country. But take the long way. The Sunshine Coast run starts by passing the Glass House Mountains, ancient volcanic plugs jutting straight out of the plains that Captain Cook named in 1770. Then Australia Zoo, the Irwin family's place, where you can hand-feed elephants. Mooloolaba has a patrolled beach and the underwater world aquarium. And Noosa is the finish, national park headland walks, a river mouth, and Hastings Street lined with restaurants. Winter here is dry, sunny and mild while the south freezes. This stretch of Queensland is built for slow mornings. When was the last time you swam in the ocean in July?
whyTrending: The Sunshine Coast enjoys warm, dry winters (June-August) while southern Australia is cold, making it a top winter-escape drive. Australia Zoo and Noosa National Park are enduring draws, and the Glass House Mountains lookouts trend on 2026 short-getaway content.

title: Darwin to Katherine on the Nature's Way loop
subtitle: 280 miles by road
script: You could drive from Darwin all the way to Katherine, 280 miles, on about 35 dollars of gas, and swim under waterfalls that vanish half the year. This is Nature's Way. You leave Darwin for Kakadu, nearly 20,000 square kilometres of wetlands, escarpments and 20,000-year-old rock art you can walk right up to. Jim Jim Falls plunges 200 metres off the Arnhem escarpment into a plunge pool you can swim in once the dry season locks in. Then south to Nitmiluk, thirteen sandstone gorges you paddle by canoe near Katherine. Go May to October, the dry, when the roads open and the crocs stay put. This is the Top End at its wildest. Could you handle a swim below a 200-metre waterfall?
whyTrending: The Top End dry season (May-October) is when Kakadu's 4WD tracks to Jim Jim Falls open and the waterholes become swimmable. Kakadu's ancient rock art and Nitmiluk canoeing anchor the NT's 2026 dry-season itineraries as the wetlands drain and wildlife concentrates.

title: Adelaide to Kangaroo Island via the Fleurieu Peninsula
subtitle: 80 miles by road
script: Did you know for about 10 dollars in gas you could drive 80 miles from Adelaide to Kingscote, then a short ferry to an island where wildlife outnumbers people ten to one? The Fleurieu run kicks off in McLaren Vale, one of the oldest wine regions in Australia, grenache in every glass. Victor Harbor has a horse-drawn tram out to Granite Island. Cape Jervis is where the ferry to Kangaroo Island departs. On the island itself, sea lions haul out on the beach at Seal Bay and koalas hang in the gums. Southern right whales cruise the Fleurieu coast from May to October. Spring means wildflowers and baby wildlife everywhere. This is South Australia doing what it does best, slow and wild. Would you share a beach with a colony of sea lions?
whyTrending: Kangaroo Island has rebounded strongly since the 2020 bushfires and its Seal Bay sea-lion colony is a major wildlife draw. Southern right whales pass the Fleurieu coast May-October, and spring brings wildflowers and newborn wildlife, lifting the route on 2026 SA travel lists.

title: Cairns to Airlie Beach on the Whitsundays coast
subtitle: 390 miles by road
script: 390 miles from Cairns to Airlie Beach, and it costs you just 50 dollars in gas to reach the gateway to the whitest sand in the world. This is the run down to the Whitsundays. You leave Cairns and reach Mission Beach, where the rainforest spills onto the sand and cassowaries roam. Townsville is the sunniest city in Queensland, with Magnetic Island a short ferry away. Bowen gave the world its mangoes and some seriously quiet beaches. And Airlie Beach is where you board a boat to Whitehaven, 98 percent pure silica sand that squeaks under your feet. Dry season, May to October, means blue skies and calm seas the whole way. This is the tropical Queensland everyone screenshots. Which beach would you sail to first?
whyTrending: The Whitsundays and Whitehaven Beach are among Australia's most photographed spots, and the dry season (May-October) delivers calm seas and clear skies ideal for sailing. Townsville and Magnetic Island are growing budget-travel hubs, keeping this tropical coast run popular in 2026.

title: Melbourne to Sydney on the Sapphire Coast alpine way
subtitle: 640 miles by road
script: For about 80 dollars of gas you can cross 640 miles from Melbourne to Sydney along the coast, and see a side of Australia the people who fly never will. You leave Melbourne through Gippsland to Lakes Entrance, the largest inland waterway system in the country. Cross into New South Wales and hit Merimbula on the Sapphire Coast, oyster leases and dolphin-filled bays. Batemans Bay is where the Clyde River meets the sea and kangaroos graze the foreshore at dusk. Then the road climbs to Sydney past empty white-sand beaches the tour buses skip entirely. Autumn gives you mild days and roads to yourself. This is the long way, and it's the only way worth doing. Would you take three days instead of ninety minutes?
whyTrending: The Sapphire Coast between Melbourne and Sydney is the uncrowded alternative to the inland Hume Highway, with Merimbula oysters and dolphin bays. Autumn (March-May) offers mild weather and quiet roads, and the drive is gaining traction in 2026 as a slow-travel alternative to flying.

title: Broome to Exmouth on the Coral Coast
subtitle: 780 miles by road
script: You could drive from Broome all the way to Exmouth, 780 miles across the most remote coast in Australia, on about 100 dollars of gas, and swim beside a whale shark the size of a bus. This is the run from Broome to Exmouth. You leave Cable Beach and its famous camel-lined sunsets, pass the iron-ore giant Port Hedland, then detour inland to Karijini, where billion-year-old gorges drop into swimming holes so deep they're black. Back on the coast, Coral Bay puts a fringing reef fifty metres off the sand. And Exmouth sits beside Ningaloo, the reef where whale sharks gather every autumn and winter. Go March to July for whale shark season and dry roads. This is Western Australia at its most jaw-dropping and least visited. Would you get in the water with the biggest fish on Earth?
whyTrending: Ningaloo Reef's whale shark season runs roughly March-July, when the world's biggest fish gather off Exmouth for one of Earth's most reliable snorkel encounters. Karijini's gorges and the fringing reef at Coral Bay make WA's Coral Coast a marquee 2026 adventure drive.

title: Queenstown to Milford Sound: The Fiordland Run
subtitle: 179 miles by road
script: 179 miles from Queenstown to Milford Sound, and it costs you just 25 dollars in gas to reach a fiord that gets 182 rainy days a year, which locals swear is exactly when you want to be there. Waterfalls explode off every cliff. Start in Queenstown, drive two hours to Te Anau, then turn onto the Milford Road. Stop at Mirror Lakes for the postcard reflection, roll through the golden Eglinton Valley, then walk five minutes into The Chasm where the river drills through solid rock. The final tunnel spits you out at the fiord itself, Mitre Peak rising straight from black water. Fill your tank in Te Anau, there's none on the road ahead. Summer cruise slots book out weeks ahead now. Would you chase the sun here, or the storm?
whyTrending: Milford Sound cruise slots and the Milford Road are peak-season crushed December through February, pushing 2026 visitors toward shoulder months. Locals increasingly recommend visiting on rainy days when hundreds of temporary waterfalls appear across the fiord walls. The remote Milford Road has no fuel for 119km, a detail going viral in trip-planning videos.

title: Queenstown to Wanaka over the Crown Range
subtitle: 43 miles by road
script: For about 5 dollars of gas you can cross 43 miles from Queenstown to Wanaka, but the road climbs 1,120 metres over the Crown Range, the highest sealed main road in New Zealand, turning a simple hop into the best hour of your trip. Leave Queenstown and detour into Arrowtown, a preserved gold-rush village where the autumn trees go copper in April. Then the road starts climbing, switchback after switchback, until you hit the Crown Range summit lookout and the whole Wakatipu Basin drops away behind you. Coast down to Cardrona, stop at the pub that's poured beers since 1863, and roll into Wanaka by its famous lone tree in the lake. Snow closes the pass some winter mornings. Which season are you driving it?
whyTrending: The Crown Range is New Zealand's highest main road and a bucket-list drive between two of Otago's most-visited towns. Arrowtown's autumn colours in April draw a dedicated Autumn Festival crowd, while winter snow occasionally closes the pass, making seasonal timing a hot 2026 planning topic. The historic 1863 Cardrona Hotel is one of NZ's most photographed pubs.

title: Christchurch to Aoraki Mount Cook
subtitle: 205 miles by road
destination: Aoraki Mount Cook Village, New Zealand
script: For about 25 dollars in gas you can drive all 205 miles from Christchurch to the tallest mountain in the country, and it's the best money you'll spend in New Zealand. From Christchurch it's four hours across the Canterbury Plains into the Mackenzie Basin. First stop, Lake Tekapo, where the tiny stone Church of the Good Shepherd sits under a certified Dark Sky reserve. Keep going to Lake Pukaki, that unreal milky-turquoise water fed straight off the glaciers. Detour into Twizel for pies, then the final road hugs Pukaki's shore straight toward Aoraki, 3,724 metres of it, filling your entire windscreen. Summer nights here are packed for stargazing tours. Could you sleep through a sky like that?
whyTrending: The Mackenzie region is a certified International Dark Sky Reserve, and stargazing tours around Lake Tekapo sell out through the summer 2025-26 season. Lake Pukaki's glacier-fed turquoise water and the framed view of Aoraki/Mount Cook are among NZ's most shared images. Aoraki is New Zealand's highest peak at 3,724m.

title: Christchurch to Greymouth: Arthur's Pass Crossing
subtitle: 152 miles by road
script: 152 miles from Christchurch to Greymouth, and it costs you just 20 dollars in gas to cross the entire South Island coast to coast. One road climbs 920 metres straight through the Southern Alps, and the TranzAlpine train that shadows it is rated one of the great rail journeys on Earth. This is the Great Alpine Highway. Leave Christchurch, grab a giant donut in Springfield, then watch the plains give way to beech forest and braided rivers. Arthur's Pass Village is your alpine base, keas cheeky enough to peel the rubber off your car. Then comes the Otira Viaduct, an engineering feat clinging to the mountainside, before west-coast rainforest drops you into Greymouth on the wild Tasman Sea. Snow can chain the road in winter. East coast or west, which one's yours?
whyTrending: SH73 over Arthur's Pass is NZ's most dramatic alpine crossing, paralleled by the TranzAlpine train that regularly ranks among the world's great rail journeys. Winter 2026 snow and chain requirements over the 920m pass make it a seasonal talking point. The cheeky alpine kea parrots at Arthur's Pass Village are a viral wildlife draw.

title: Nelson to Franz Josef: The Wild West Coast
subtitle: 264 miles by road
script: You could drive from Nelson all the way to Franz Josef Glacier, 264 miles, on about 35 dollars of gas. Picture a glacier that ends in temperate rainforest, 300 metres above sea level, close enough to the coast that you can drive from ice to ocean in twenty minutes. That's Franz Josef, and the road there is the wildest in the country. From Nelson you cut down to the West Coast and hit the Pancake Rocks at Punakaiki, layered limestone stacks that shoot seawater skyward through blowholes at high tide. Roll through Greymouth, then Hokitika for its glowworm dell and pounamu greenstone carvers. Finally the SH6 rainforest closes overhead and delivers you to Franz Josef township. The glacier is retreating fast, roughly the length of a rugby field each year. How long until it's gone?
whyTrending: The West Coast's SH6 links the Punakaiki Pancake Rocks blowholes with the fast-retreating Franz Josef Glacier, whose annual recession makes it a climate-urgency travel story in 2026. Hokitika's greenstone (pounamu) carving and glowworm dell are cultural draws, and the Hokitika Wildfoods Festival every March spikes visitor interest. Franz Josef is one of few glaciers on Earth to descend into rainforest.

title: Dunedin to Invercargill: The Catlins Coast
subtitle: 143 miles by road
script: Did you know for about 20 dollars in gas you could drive 143 miles from Dunedin to Invercargill? There's a forest here that turned to stone 180 million years ago, and at low tide you can walk right across it. Welcome to the Catlins, the loneliest stretch of the Southern Scenic Route. From Dunedin you drop south to Nugget Point, where a lighthouse guards a chain of rocky islets crawling with fur seals and sea lions. Cut inland to Purakaunui Falls, a three-tiered curtain framed by native bush, then reach Curio Bay and that petrified Jurassic forest, one of the most complete on the planet. Rare yellow-eyed penguins waddle ashore here at dusk. Finish in Invercargill, the southernmost city in the country. When did you last have a coast to yourself?
whyTrending: The Catlins section of the Southern Scenic Route remains one of NZ's least-crowded coastlines, appealing to 2026 travellers seeking uncrowded alternatives to Queenstown and Milford. Curio Bay's 180-million-year-old petrified forest and the endangered yellow-eyed penguin (hoiho) that nest there are major wildlife draws. Nugget Point's lighthouse and sea-lion colony are among the South's most photographed spots.

title: Kaikoura to Christchurch: Whales and Vineyards
subtitle: 112 miles by road
script: Just 15 bucks of gas covers all 112 miles from Kaikoura to Christchurch, one of the most wildlife-packed drives on Earth. Sperm whales the length of a bus surface just three kilometres off this coast, every single day of the year, because a deep ocean trench meets the shore right here. That's why you start in Kaikoura. Before you leave, pull in at Ohau Point where hundreds of fur seal pups tumble down to a hidden waterfall pool in winter. Then State Highway One hugs the Pacific south, rebuilt tougher after the 2016 quake lifted this whole seabed. Roll through the farming town of Cheviot, then swing into the Waipara Valley, North Canterbury's rising wine region, for a cool-climate Pinot before Christchurch. Whale tours fill fast in calm summer seas. Land, sea, or a glass in hand, what's your stop?
whyTrending: Kaikoura is one of the only places on Earth to see resident sperm whales year-round thanks to an offshore deep-sea trench, and calm summer 2026 seas book whale tours out fast. The Ohau Point seal-pup waterfall is a viral winter wildlife spectacle. The Waipara Valley wine region is gaining recognition for cool-climate Pinot Noir, adding a food-and-wine angle to the coastal drive.

title: Auckland to Cathedral Cove: Coromandel Peninsula
subtitle: 120 miles by road
script: For about 15 dollars of gas you can cross 120 miles from Auckland to Hahei on the Coromandel. Bring a spade. At Hot Water Beach you literally dig your own hot tub in the sand, because a geothermal spring bubbles up right under the shore two hours either side of low tide. This is the Coromandel, and it's an easy escape from Auckland. Cross the Hauraki Plains to Thames, the old gold-mining gateway, then wind up the coast to Coromandel Town for smoked mussels and craft beer. Cut over the ranges to Hot Water Beach, dig in, then finish at Hahei, the launch point for Cathedral Cove, that towering sea arch you've seen in a hundred films. Summer weekends turn this peninsula into gridlock. Would you go now, or wait for a quiet autumn?
whyTrending: The Coromandel is Auckland's classic summer escape, and Hot Water Beach's dig-your-own hot pool is one of NZ's most shared beach experiences. Cathedral Cove, reopened and repaired after storm damage, is back on 2026 must-see lists after appearing in major films. Summer weekend congestion is pushing travellers to consider quieter autumn timing.

title: Auckland to Paihia: Bay of Islands
subtitle: 145 miles by road
script: 145 miles from Auckland to Paihia, and it costs you just 20 dollars in gas to reach the spot where modern New Zealand began. 144 islands scatter across one turquoise bay in the subtropical winterless north, three hours from the city. Break the drive at Whangarei to walk behind the Whangarei Falls, then stop in Kawakawa for the wildly artistic Hundertwasser public toilets, genuinely the most photographed loos in the country. Push on to Waitangi, where Maori chiefs and the British Crown signed the founding treaty in 1840, a place every Kiwi learns about in school. Finish in Paihia, gateway to dolphin cruises and the Hole in the Rock. Waitangi Day every February 6th draws huge crowds. Ready to stand where the nation was born?
whyTrending: The Bay of Islands is the birthplace of the Treaty of Waitangi (1840), and Waitangi Day on February 6 draws large 2026 crowds to the Treaty Grounds. Kawakawa's Hundertwasser-designed public toilets are a quirky viral landmark, and the subtropical winterless north offers warm shoulder-season travel. Dolphin cruises and the Hole in the Rock remain top Northland draws.

title: Auckland to Taupo: Volcanic Heartland
subtitle: 173 miles by road
script: You could drive from Auckland all the way to Taupo, 173 miles, on about 20 dollars of gas. And here's your finish line: 220,000 litres of water blast over Huka Falls every single second, enough to fill an Olympic pool in eleven seconds flat. This is the volcanic guts of the North Island. From Auckland it's a straight shot to Hamilton and its award-winning riverside gardens. Then swing into Rotorua, where the whole town smells of sulphur, geysers erupt on cue and Maori cultural villages share stories over a hangi cooked in the earth. Carry on to Huka Falls, feel that turquoise torrent through your chest, then reach Lake Taupo, a lake sitting inside a supervolcano bigger than Singapore. Summer holidays pack the lakefront. Adventure capital of the north, are you in?
whyTrending: This route strings together the North Island's geothermal core, with Rotorua's geysers and Maori cultural experiences and Taupo's supervolcano lake drawing steady 2026 interest. Huka Falls' 220,000-litre-per-second flow is a signature viral visual. Lake Taupo, sitting in one of the world's largest calderas, positions itself as the North Island's adventure capital.

title: Taupo to Napier: Thermal Explorer to Art Deco
subtitle: 89 miles by road
script: For about 10 dollars of gas you can cross 89 miles from Taupo to Napier. One earthquake in 1931 flattened an entire city, and locals rebuilt it in the flashiest style of the day, so today Napier is one of the finest Art Deco towns on the planet. But first, the drive. From Taupo, start at Wairakei where steam hisses out of the ground and a geothermal power station taps the heat below. Climb into the hills past Tarawera, then descend through Te Pohue as sheep country rolls out toward the sea. You land in Napier and its Hawke's Bay wine country, straight-line streets lined with pastel 1930s facades. The Art Deco Festival every February fills the streets with vintage cars and jazz. Grab a Syrah and step back a century, deal?
whyTrending: Napier was rebuilt entirely in Art Deco style after the devastating 1931 Hawke's Bay earthquake, and its Art Deco Festival every February draws big crowds in vintage dress and cars. The Thermal Explorer Highway from Taupo passes the Wairakei geothermal field. Hawke's Bay is one of NZ's premier wine regions, especially for Syrah and Chardonnay.

title: Wellington to Napier: Wairarapa Wine Trail
subtitle: 200 miles by road
script: Roughly 25 dollars in gas takes you the 200 miles from Wellington's harbour to Art Deco Napier, through two of the North Island's best wine regions in one shot. Leave the capital and climb the Remutaka hill road, then drop into Martinborough, a village laid out like a Union Jack where you can cycle between world-class Pinot Noir cellar doors. Push on to Masterton in the Wairarapa heartland, then cross to Woodville and thread the Manawatu Gorge country. The road opens into Hawke's Bay and the pastel streets of Napier, rebuilt in pure 1930s style after its famous quake. Harvest season from March fills the vineyards and the calendar. Two wine regions, one tank of gas, when are you going?
whyTrending: This route links two celebrated wine regions, Martinborough's Pinot Noir in the Wairarapa and Napier's Hawke's Bay, with the March-onward harvest season a key 2026 draw. Martinborough's Union Jack street layout makes it ideal for cellar-door cycling. Napier's Art Deco architecture, rebuilt after the 1931 quake, anchors the northern end.
